title: Refactor liblex tests to direct State unit tests

content.text:
In @src/liblex.zig tests, I tested logic of functions of State through their wrappers. I changed my mind, I want to tests them directly as unit tests.

- Change `lex_*` used in tests to `State.*` relevant function.
- Update the test name accordingly.
- Strictly keep the semantic and other parts of the tests.

Let me preview your plan.

content.thinking: I need to examine the file to see what I'm working with.

content.text:
## Plan

Replace `lex_*` calls inside tests with direct `State.*` method calls and rename test labels accordingly. Semantics, arrange/act/assert structure, comments, and assertions remain unchanged.

### Replacements

- `lex_state_init(&state)` → `state.init()`
- `lex_add(&state, c)` → `state.add(c)`
- `lex_backspace(&state)` → `state.backspace()`

For the first test that uses raw allocation:
- `lex_state_init(state)` → `state.init()`
- `lex_add(state, c)` → `state.add(c)`
- Keep `lex_state_size` and `lex_state_alignment` since those are the export constants being tested for ABI allocation; or replace with `@sizeOf(State)` / `@alignOf(State)` for full directness.

Question: For the raw-allocation test (line 470), should I:
- **(a)** Keep `lex_state_size`/`lex_state_alignment` (only swap the two function calls), or
- **(b)** Replace them with `@sizeOf(State)`/`@alignOf(State)` to make it fully a `State` unit test?

### Test renames

- `"expect lex_add handles non-Telex characters less than buffer_effective range"` → `"expect State.add handles non-Telex characters less than buffer_effective range"`
- `"expect lex_add handles non-Telex characters exceed the buffer_effective range"` → `"expect State.add handles non-Telex characters exceed the buffer_effective range"`
- `"expect lex_add adds a literally because it's the start of the buffer"` → `"expect State.add adds a literally because it's the start of the buffer"`
- `"expect lex_add start literal input when the new input just exceeds buffer_effective"` → `"expect State.add start literal input when the new input just exceeds buffer_effective"`
- `"expect lex_add results â when input aa"` → `"expect State.add results â when input aa"`
- `"expect lex_add results aA when input aaA"` → `"expect State.add results aA when input aaA"`
- `"expect lex_backspace will reduce buffer_length by 1 and won't touch literal_index"` → `"expect State.backspace will reduce buffer_length by 1 and won't touch literal_index"`
- `"expect lex_backspace will reduce buffer_length by 1 and unset literal_index"` → `"expect State.backspace will reduce buffer_length by 1 and unset literal_index"`
- `"expect lex_backspace will reduce buffer_length by 1 when literal_index is not set"` → `"expect State.backspace will reduce buffer_length by 1 when literal_index is not set"`

Awaiting confirmation on (a) vs (b) before applying.

My comments:

- For the raw-allocation test -> Create a new test: "Expect State can be initialized by raw allocation", update existing tests to use State.init.

Update these tests.

content.thinking: I need to write a new test for raw allocation and review the rest of the file to make sure I have the full picture of what's being tested.

result.output:

content.text:
All 14 tests pass. Changes:

- Added new test `"Expect State can be initialized by raw allocation"` keeping the raw-allocation flow with `lex_state_init`/`lex_add`.
- Renamed `lex_add` tests to `State.add` and `lex_backspace` tests to `State.backspace`, swapping wrapper calls for direct method calls (`state.init()`, `state.add(c)`, `state.backspace()`). Semantics, comments, and assertions unchanged.
